retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ubiquitination -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: These ubiquitination reactions act as a signal for endocytosis and transporting cellular vesicles to lysosomes. Disruptions in this pathway have implications in many oncogenic formations. Ubiquitination of membrane protein trafficking has also shown associations with viruses, such as Ebola and HIV, that make their way to the cell surface after replicating within the cell. [8] [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Mallory Bodies -- Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: The 2nd mechanism centers on a shift in protein degradation pathways. Under normal conditions, the 26S proteasome clears intracellular proteins, including cytosolic, nuclear, and membrane proteins. However, stressors like 3,5-diethoxycarbonyl-1,4-dihydrocollidine (DDC) or alcohol disrupt this system, prompting a shift from the 26S proteasome to the immunoproteasome. This change impairs protein clearance, leading to intracellular protein accumulation. When molecular chaperones, such as heat shock protein 70, fail to manage misfolded proteins, these aggregates are tagged with ubiquitin and directed toward degradation via the p62 pathway. If this system is overwhelmed or dysfunctional, inclusion bodies such as MDBs begin to form.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ubiquitination -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Ubiquitin is an evolutionarily conserved protein found in nearly all eukaryotic organisms. It is highly conserved and virtually identical across all life forms, whether human, yeast, or plant. Ubiquitin is a 76 amino acid protein. The main features of ubiquitin are its 7 lysine (Lys) residues and the N-terminus. Ubiquitin can be ubiquitinated on any of the 7 Lys residues or on the N-terminus. Lys48- and Lys63-linked chains are the most common polyubiquitination sites, which could lead to protein degradation and endocytosis, respectively. [1] [3] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Phagocytosis -- Issues of Concern -- Receptor-led Formation of the Phagosome
retrieved_docs.content: As the phagosome traverses in the direction of the phagocyte’s centrosome, it fuses with a lysosome containing degradative enzymes such as proteases and iron-sequestering molecules such as lactoferrin. This fused organelle is termed the phagolysosome and gradually acidifies to activate proteolytic and degradative enzymes. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Mallory Bodies -- Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: The 3rd mechanism involves chronic activation of toll-like receptors (TLRs), which stimulate proinflammatory and cell growth pathways. Inflammatory cytokines, such as tumor necrosis factor α (TNF-α) and interferon γ (IFN-γ), activate TLR signaling, promoting the upregulation of the immunoproteasome and downregulation of the 26S proteasome. This imbalance further hinders protein degradation and contributes to the accumulation of MDB. Agents such as DDC not only disrupt proteasome function but also enhance TLR signaling by increasing tumor necrosis factor α and interferon γ expression, creating a feedback loop that amplifies cellular stress and MDB formation. [21] [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ubiquitination -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Ubiquitination is a 3-step process involving 3 enzymes: ubiquitin-activating enzyme (E1), ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme (E2), and ubiquitin-protein ligase (E3). E1 activates ubiquitin in an ATP-dependent manner, enabling its transfer onto the ubiquitin carrier enzyme, E2. Activated ubiquitin is then transferred by the ubiquitin protein ligase, E3, to a substrate protein. Monoubiquitination acts as a signal for nonproteolytic events such as endocytosis, histone regulation, DNA repair, virus budding, and nuclear export. Ubiquitin can also be attached to a target protein in polyubiquitination, where multiple ubiquitin molecules are attached to a single lysine (Lys) residue. Ubiquitin contains 7 different lysine residues that can potentially be used for ubiquitin-chain extension.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ubiquitination -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The ubiquitin-proteasome is a well-studied pathway that plays a significant role in regulating protein homeostasis and trafficking. Ubiquitination regulates various cellular processes, including immune response, angiogenesis, cell proliferation, apoptosis, and DNA repair. Any given protein could be ubiquitinated at multiple sites with a different effect on the target protein. Targeting a specific ubiquitination site is proven to be challenging. Although various drugs (eg, bortezomib) have been developed to inhibit the proteasome pathway, their non-specific therapeutic applications are limited due to their non-specific activities. E3 ligases often have multiple substrates with diverse functions. Inhibiting an E3 ligase may prevent the ubiquitination of other proteins that are not of point of interest, resulting in an unintended or off-target effect.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ubiquitination -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ubiquitination plays a crucial role in virtually every aspect of cellular functions. One of the best-understood functions of ubiquitination is protein degradation. This pathway targets proteins to the proteasome, which degrades and recycles the proteins. It has many functions, including cell signaling, apoptosis, protein processing, immune response, and DNA repair.

retrieved_docs.title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The degradation function of lysosomes has three components. Among them, phagocytosis and endocytosis concern extracellular particles. The former leads to the formation of a phagosome and the latter an endosome. The process of degrading endogenous substances is called autophagy. It results in an autophagosome that is then extruded out of the cell by exocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Microscopy, Light -- Lysosomes
retrieved_docs.content: Lysosomes are vesicles that pinch off from the Golgi apparatus. These structures contain enzymes that degrade and recycle cellular waste through a process known as autophagy. This mechanism eliminates unwanted molecules and foreign particles, such as bacteria and viruses, that enter the cell. Nearly 60 different lysosomal hydrolases, the chemicals responsible for this process, have been identified. [28] Thus, lysosomes are also called "digestive bags" or "cellular garbage disposal units."

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ubiquitination -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Ubiquitination is a tightly regulated, highly specific, and ATP-dependent biological process carried out by a complex cascade of enzymes. Ubiquitination is an essential player in protein homeostasis, serving to remove unwanted or damaged proteins rapidly. The ubiquitination pathway is involved in the regulation of many basic cellular processes, including cell division and differentiation, response to environmental stress, cell differentiation, immune response, DNA repair, and apoptosis. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ubiquitination -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Although targeting protein ubiquitination has proven challenging, new therapeutic strategies are expected to emerge as we learn more about the ubiquitination pathway. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Lewy Bodies -- Biochemical and Genetic Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: In summary, the Ub-proteasome system and the autophagy pathway help our brain clear toxic forms of misaggregated proteins, including helping with a-Syn turnover. Dysfunctional proteolytic pathways may result in the accumulation of toxic forms of a-Syn. Ubiquitinated proteins in LBs may be a manifestation of attempts by the cellular machinery to eliminate damaged cellular components and delay the onset of neuronal degeneration. Some evidence showing this is the high incidence of mitochondrial DNA deletion in LB-positive neurons in PD brain compared to LB-negative neurons and controls, suggesting increased mitochondrial damage in LB-positive neurons. [24] [25] These deletions trigger neuroprotective mechanisms that at one point may become overwhelmed. However, how this process works in the mitochondria, specifically how soluble converts to insoluble aggregates in neurons is poorly understood and requires further research.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ubiquitination -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The ubiquitination pathway is key in many cellular signaling pathways. For example, polyubiquitin of IkB-alpha plays a central role in the signal activation of IkB-alpha. The nuclear factor-kappa B (NF-κB) signaling pathway regulates various biological functions in the body. Its aberrant activation can lead to the pathogenesis of diseases, such as respiratory diseases and cancers. IκBα ubiquitination occurs when IκBα is phosphorylated at Ser32/Ser36. Phosphorylation of Ser32/Ser36 allows certain ubiquitin E3 ligases to recognize IκBα. Ubiquitinated IκBα is then recognized by the proteasome complex, which leads to its degradation. Degradation of IkB-alpha results in the release of the transcription factor NFkB to the nucleus, which causes an inflammatory response. As mentioned above, protein ubiquitination also plays a role in protein trafficking (eg, endocytosis). For instance, proteins modified by monoubiquitination and K63-linked polyubiquitination can control proteins' activity, interactions, and subcellular distribution.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Catabolism -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Lysosomal Degradation: Whether proteins are no longer needed or synthesized incorrectly, intracellular proteins are broken down using lysosomes and the proteasome complex. Proteins enter the lysosome through autophagy, a highly regulated process in which vesicles fuse with the lysosome membrane. [10] Autophagy can be promoted by low energy states such as low ATP or high AMP levels within the cell. Because AMP-activated protein kinase becomes activated with high AMP levels, it can phosphorylate tuberous sclerosis complex 1 and 2, activating Rheb-GTPase. [11] This activation is important because it inactivates Rheb and, thus, inactivates mTOR, a major autophagy inhibitor; this is how low-energy states favor protein catabolism. Conversely, insulin activating the tuberous sclerosis complex 1 and 2 through Akt kinase leads to active mTOR. [11] Activated mTOR favors protein synthesis rather than degradation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2594
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas as a broader category that includes more specific proteasome‑inhibiting agents. The entity inhibitory proteas is identified as ixazomib, indicating that ixazomib is a particular proteasome inhibitor falling under this category. Additionally, remikiren is classified as a type of inhibitory proteas, meaning that remikiren also belongs to the same class of proteasome‑inhibiting compounds associated with ixazomib. Together, amprenavir, inhibitory proteas, ixazomib, and remikiren illustrate a hierarchical relationship among proteasome inhibitors, with amprenavir encompassing the broader class, inhibitory proteas representing the specific class, and ixazomib and remikiren being individual agents within that class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1982
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir, hirudiny, ixazomib, and remikiren are all connected through the concept of inhibitory proteas (proteasome inhibition). Amprenavir has a child entity identified as inhibitory proteas, indicating that a proteasome‑inhibiting component is derived from or associated with amprenavir. Hirudiny is classified as an inhibitory proteas, meaning it functions as a proteasome inhibitor. The inhibitory proteas class is linked to ixazomib, a clinically used proteasome inhibitor, suggesting that inhibitory proteas share mechanistic or structural characteristics with ixazomib. Additionally, remikiren is positioned as a subordinate entity within the inhibitory proteas hierarchy, indicating that remikiren is a specific proteasome‑inhibiting agent related to the broader inhibitory proteas category. Together, these relationships illustrate how various drugs and compounds are organized around the central pharmacologic theme of proteasome inhibition, which is relevant for therapeutic strategies targeting protein degradation pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 29
retrieved_docs.content: inverse‑is‑a link), highlighting their role in modulating proteasomal function. In the antifungal domain, the triazole derivatives itraconazole, flukonazol and voriconazole (vorikonazol) are grouped as systemic antimycotics, reflecting their shared therapeutic use against fungal infections. Collectively, these relationships emphasize the importance of recognizing drug class memberships, specific contraindications, and overlapping mechanisms—particularly CYP3A4‑mediated metabolism, protease inhibition, and proteasome inhibition—to optimize patient safety and therapeutic efficacy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is connected to an entity called inhibitory proteas, which is classified as ixazomib; thus, the inhibitory proteas component associated with amprenavir is identified as the proteasome‑inhibiting drug ixazomib.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: fungal infections. Collectively, the community’s knowledge emphasizes that alfuzosin, alprazolam and lovastatin should not be used concurrently with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors—including protease inhibitors, direct‑acting antivirals, and systemic triazole antifungals—to avoid reduced therapeutic efficacy, heightened toxicity, and serious adverse events, and it highlights the shared pharmacologic theme of proteasome inhibition among certain antiviral and proteasome‑targeting agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the antiviral agents ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir and tipranavir. Within this class, amprenavir is linked to a subcategory termed inhibitory proteas, and the broader protease‑inhibitor class is also associated with the inhibitory proteas concept. Inhibitory proteas is identified as the proteasome‑inhibiting drug ixazomib, and it encompasses the specific agent remikiren. These hierarchical and functional connections illustrate how antiviral protease inhibitors relate to proteasome‑targeting compounds, underscoring shared mechanistic themes that are important for drug classification, therapeutic decision‑making and the development of treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
retrieved_docs.content: fosamprenavir, tipranavir and other listed agents belong to the “Protease inhibitors, direct‑acting antivirals” class, a group linked to broader proteasome‑inhibitory activity; amprenavir is noted as a child of the “inhibitory proteas” concept. The “inhibitory proteas” category comprises proteasome inhibitors such as ixazomib and hirudiny, and an inverse ISA relationship connects this category with remikiren, placing remikiren within a pharmacologic group associated with proteasome inhibition. The “Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics” class includes itraconazole, fluconazole, posaconazole and voriconazole, all sharing systemic antifungal activity; posaconazole is additionally classified as a trypanocidal agent used to treat infections caused by Trypanosoma species. These interconnections highlight the clinical necessity of thorough medication reconciliation to avoid dangerous drug‑drug interactions among alpha‑adrenergic antagonists, benzodiazepines, statins, protease

retrieved_docs.content: Nemoc is associated with اضطراب استقلاب الأحماض الأمينية, an amino‑acid metabolism disorder, indicating that individuals with nemoc may exhibit disturbances in amino‑acid metabolic pathways, which is relevant for diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 755

Together, these agents illustrate distinct pharmacologic classes—α‑1‑adrenergic antagonism, benzodiazepine sedation, HMG‑CoA reductase inhibition, proteasome inhibition, and triazole antifungal therapy—defined by their molecular targets and therapeutic applications, highlighting the clinical importance of mechanism‑based drug classification for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitory proteas refers to a class of proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Ixazomib is a specific drug that belongs to this inhibitory proteas class, acting as a reversible proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. Remikiren, although primarily recognized as a renin inhibitor, is also categorized within the inhibitory proteas group because it exhibits proteasome‑inhibitory activity, placing it alongside ixazomib as another member of this therapeutic class. This relationship highlights that both ixazomib and remikiren share a common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, despite their differing primary indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1091
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that are direct‑acting antivirals comprise the agents fosamprenavir, amprenavir and tipranavir. Amprenavir is linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, indicating that it functions as a proteasome‑inhibiting compound. The broader class of protease inhibitors, direct‑acting antivirals, also has a relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting that members of this class commonly exhibit proteasome‑inhibitory activity. In addition, hirudiny is classified as an inhibitory proteas, and remikiren is identified as an inhibitory proteas as well. Together, these connections underscore the role of various antiviral protease inhibitors and related molecules in targeting proteasomal pathways, which is relevant for therapeutic strategies aimed at viral replication and proteasome‑mediated processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: antiparasitic, and supportive pharmacologic agents, highlighting shared mechanisms such as decreased protein synthesis, overlapping chemical families (imidazole, triazole, nitroimidazole), and dual therapeutic roles that are essential for informed therapeutic decision‑making and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2174
retrieved_docs.content: Hirudiny is classified as an inhibitory proteas, indicating that it functions as a proteasome‑inhibiting agent. The class of inhibitory proteas is linked to ixazomib, a well‑known proteasome inhibitor drug, and is also related to remikiren, which is identified as a member of this inhibitory proteas category. Together, hirudiny, ixazomib, and remikiren share the common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, a therapeutic strategy employed in conditions such as certain cancers where disruption of protein degradation pathways can be beneficial.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: members exert proteasome‑inhibitory effects, and amprenavir is specifically associated with the child class “inhibitory proteas”. The inhibitory proteasome category comprises the proteasome inhibitor ixazomib, is inversely related to remikiren (another proteasome‑targeting compound), and includes Hirudiny as a member. In the antifungal domain, the systemic antimycotics class of triazole derivatives includes itraconazole, flukonazol and vorikonazol, with flukonazol sharing the contraindication with cisapride. Recognizing these class‑based mechanisms, CYP3A4 inhibition, and specific drug‑interaction warnings is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 358
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors that function as direct‑acting antivirals comprise fosamprenavir, amprenavir, indinavir and tipranavir, linking these agents to the broader class of protease‑targeted antiviral therapy. Amprenavir is specifically associated with an “inhibitory proteas” entity, indicating that it may possess proteasome‑inhibitory properties, and the entire class of protease inhibitors, direct‑acting antivirals, is described as having a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting overlap between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition mechanisms. Hirudiny is identified as an instance of inhibitory proteas, reinforcing its role as a proteasome‑inhibiting compound. The inhibitory proteas concept is further classified as a type of ixazomib, placing ixazomib within the proteasome‑inhibitor family, and it is also noted as a subclass of remikiren, indicating that remikiren represents a broader category that encompasses these proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Together, these connections illustrate how antiviral protease inhibitors and proteasome inhibitors intersect, which can inform therapeutic strategies that target both viral replication and protein degradation pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 402
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a therapeutic class that includes the specific agents fosamprenavir, atazanavir and tipranavir, each of which is a member of this antiviral protease‑inhibiting group. Beyond their antiviral activity, protease inhibitors share a broader pharmacologic relationship with compounds that act as inhibitory proteas, linking viral protease inhibition to proteasome inhibition pathways. Within the inhibitory proteas category, both hirudiny and ixazomib function as proteasome‑inhibiting agents, illustrating that they are types of inhibitory proteas. Remikiren is associated with the inhibitory proteas class through an inverse relationship, suggesting that it is not a direct member but rather a molecule whose activity is counter‑regulated or targeted by proteasome inhibition. This network of relationships underscores the overlap between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition, providing insight into shared mechanisms that may influence drug selection, combination therapy, and potential repurposing strategies in patient care.
